html:
[1043]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 會青州太守病嗽 - Formal statement introducing the context, meaning 'When the governor of Qingzhou fell ill with cough.'
  • 牒檄所属征医 - Means 'issued a document to summon doctors from the subordinate areas.'
  • 沂故山僻少医工 - Means 'Yi, being a remote mountainous place, had few medical practitioners.'
  • 令惧无以塞责 - Means 'The magistrate feared he could not fulfill his duty.'
  • 责里中使自报 - Means 'He ordered the villagers to report themselves.'
  • 共举张 - Means 'They all recommended Zhang.'
  • 立召之 - Means 'Immediately summoned him.'
  • 张方痰喘不能自疗 - Means 'Zhang, who was just suffering from coughing and shortness of breath, could not treat himself.'
  • 闻命大惧，固辞 - Means 'Upon hearing the order, he was greatly afraid and firmly declined.'
  • 令弗听，卒邮送之去 - Means 'The magistrate did not listen and sent him off by post.'
  • 路经深山，渴极 - Means 'On the way through the deep mountains, he was extremely thirsty.'
  • 咳愈甚 - Means 'His cough became even worse.'
  • 入村求水，而出中水价与玉液等 - Means 'He entered a village to ask for water, but the price of water there was as expensive as jade liquor.'
  • 遍乞之无与者 - Means 'He asked everyone for water, but no one gave him any.'
  • 见一妇漉野菜 - Means 'He saw a woman filtering wild vegetables.'
  • 菜多水寡，盎中浓浊如涎 - Means 'There were many vegetables but little water, and the water in the pot was thick and turbid like saliva.'
  • 张燥急难堪，便乞余沈饮之 - Means 'Zhang, feeling extremely dry and anxious, begged for the leftover water to drink.'
  • 少间渴解，嗽亦顿止 - Means 'After a while, his thirst was quenched, and his cough stopped abruptly.'
  • 阴念：殆良方也 - Means 'He secretly thought: It must be a good remedy.'
  • 比至郡，诸邑医工已先施治，并未痊减 - Means 'By the time he arrived at the prefecture, the doctors from various counties had already treated the governor, but the condition had not improved.'
  • 张入求密所，伪作药目，传示内外 - Means 'Zhang went inside to find a secluded place, pretended to make a prescription, and showed it to everyone inside and outside.'
  • 复遣人于民间索诸藜藿，如法淘汰讫，以汁进太守 - Means 'He then sent someone to collect various wild herbs from the people, processed them according to the method, and presented the decoction to the governor.'
  • 一服病良已 - Means 'After taking it once, the governor's illness was completely cured.'
  • 赐赉甚厚，旌以金匾 - Means 'The governor was greatly rewarded, and a golden plaque was bestowed upon him.'
  • 由此名大噪，门常如市 - Means 'As a result, his fame spread widely, and his door was always bustling with visitors.'
  • 应手无不悉效 - Means 'All treatments were effective on the spot.'
  • 有病伤寒者，言症求方 - Means 'There was a person with a cold, who described the symptoms and asked for a prescription.'
  • 张适醉，误以疟剂予之 - Means 'Zhang, who happened to be drunk, mistakenly gave him a malaria remedy.'
  • 醒而悟，不敢以告人 - Means 'When he woke up and realized his mistake, he dared not tell anyone.'
  • 三日后有盛仪造门而谢者 - Means 'Three days later, a person with a grand ceremony came to the door to thank him.'
  • 问之，则伤寒之人，大吐大下而愈矣 - Means 'When asked, it turned out that the person with a cold had been cured after a severe purging.'
  • 此类甚多 - Means 'There were many such cases.'
  • 张由此称素封，益以声价自重 - Means 'Zhang thus became known as a wealthy man and began to value his reputation more highly.'
  • 聘者非重资安舆不至焉 - Means 'Those who wanted to hire him would not come unless they offered a substantial fee and a comfortable carriage.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 青州 - One of the nine administrative divisions during the Han dynasty.
  • 太守 - Governor, a high-ranking official in charge of a prefecture.
  • 牒檄 - Official documents used for summoning or ordering.
  • 医工 - Medical practitioners.
  • 金匾 - A golden plaque, often used as a symbol of honor or reward.
  • 素封 - A wealthy man who is not a noble but has a high social status due to his wealth.

Proper Nouns

  • 青州 - A prefecture during the Han dynasty.
  • 太守 - Governor.
  • 牒檄 - Official documents.
  • 医工 - Medical practitioners.
  • 金匾 - A golden plaque.
  • 素封 - A wealthy man of high social status.

[1044]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 货药于四方 - Selling medicine everywhere.
  • 往复趾踱 - Walking back and forth.
  • 汗垢成片 - Sweat and dirt forming clumps.
  • 捻之如丸 - Can be rolled into small balls.
  • 中夜主人挝门甚急 - Knocking on the door very urgently at midnight.
  • 逾垣疾遁 - Jumped over the wall and fled quickly.
  • 藏虱 - A hidden louse.
  • 虱薄如麸 - The louse was as thin as bran.
  • 置掌中审顾之 - Placed it in the palm and examined it carefully.
  • 梦狼 - A dream about a wolf.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 益都 - A city in Shandong Province, known for its historical significance.
  • 伤寒 - A severe form of influenza, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 直隶 - A historical region in northern China, now part of Hebei Province.
  • 筮仕 - The act of seeking an official position, often through divination.
  • 南服 - Refers to the southern regions of China.
  • 无常 - In Chinese culture, a term for a messenger of death, often associated with the afterlife.

Proper Nouns

  • 益都 - A place name in Shandong Province.
  • 韩翁 - An elderly man named Han.
  • 白翁 - An elderly man named Bai.
  • 长子甲 - The eldest son of Mr. Bai, named Jia.
  • 丁姓 - A distant relative named Ding.
  • 直隶 - A historical region in northern China.

[1045]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 别后数日 - Literally 'several days after parting,' a common way to begin a narrative about events following a separation.
  • 卧 - Literally 'lying down,' but in this context, it likely means 'resting' or 'sleeping.'
  • 邀与同游 - Literally 'invited to go on a journey together,' a common phrase in Classical Chinese to describe an invitation to travel or explore.
  • 城阙 - Literally 'city gate' or 'city walls,' often used poetically to refer to a city or its grandeur.
  • 移时 - Literally 'after some time,' a phrase indicating a passage of time.
  • 此间君家甥也 - Literally 'this is your nephew in this place,' a formal way of identifying someone as a relative.
  • 蝉冠豸绣 - A formal description of an official's attire, with '蝉冠' (cicada cap) and '豸绣' (robe with a beast pattern) symbolizing high rank and authority.
  • 戟幢行列 - Literally 'halberds and banners in rows,' a description of military or ceremonial formations.
  • 无人可通 - Literally 'no one can be approached,' indicating that the place is inaccessible or that no one is available to receive visitors.
  • 曳之出 - Literally 'pulled him out,' a vivid description of being escorted or guided.
  • 衙署 - Literally 'government office,' referring to the residence or office of an official.
  • 公子 - A respectful term for a young nobleman or official.
  • 良喜 - Literally 'very happy,' a formal expression of joy.
  • 治肴蔌 - Literally 'prepare dishes,' a formal way of saying 'prepare food.'
  • 衔死人入 - Literally 'carrying a dead person inside,' a vivid and unsettling image.
  • 战惕而起 - Literally 'shaken and stood up,' a description of being startled and reacting quickly.
  • 乌在此 - Literally 'what is this,' a rhetorical question expressing surprise or disbelief.
  • 聊充庖厨 - Literally 'just to fill the kitchen,' a euphemistic way of saying 'to use as food.'
  • 辞欲出 - Literally 'wanted to leave,' a formal way of expressing the desire to depart.
  • 群狼阻道 - Literally 'a pack of wolves blocked the way,' a vivid description of being surrounded or obstructed.
  • 纷然嗥避 - Literally 'fled in a panic,' a description of animals reacting in fear.
  • 或窜床下，或伏几底 - Literally 'some hid under the bed, some crouched under the table,' a vivid description of animals seeking refuge.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翁 - Refers to an elderly man, likely the narrator or a central figure in the story.
  • 丁 - A name, likely a servant or guide in the story.
  • 晋令 - Refers to a local official in the state of Jin, indicating the setting in ancient China.
  • 白骨如山 - A vivid description of a scene of death and destruction, often used in Classical Chinese to evoke a sense of horror or desolation.
  • 狼 - A common symbol of danger and chaos in Chinese literature, often used to represent evil or malevolent forces.

Proper Nouns

  • 翁 - The narrator, an elderly man.
  • 丁 - A servant or guide in the story.
  • 晋令 - A local official in the state of Jin.
  • 公子甲 - The nephew, a young nobleman or official.

[1046]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 错愕不解其故 - To be startled and unable to understand the cause, describing a state of confusion.
  • 俄有两金甲猛士努目入 - Soon after, two golden-armored warriors with fierce eyes entered, a vivid description of the arrival of supernatural beings.
  • 出黑索索甲 - Produced black ropes to bind the armor, a metaphorical action that transforms the armor into a tiger.
  • 牙齿巉巉 - Sharp teeth, emphasizing the ferocity of the tiger.
  • 一人出利剑，欲枭其首 - One drew a sharp sword, intending to behead it, indicating the intent to kill the tiger.
  • 且勿，且勿 - Not yet, not yet, a repeated phrase used to delay an action.
  • 不如姑敲齿去 - It would be better to knock out its teeth first, suggesting a more measured approach.
  • 乃出巨锤锤齿 - They then took out a large hammer to knock out the teeth, describing the action of removing the tiger's teeth.
  • 虎大吼，声震山岳 - The tiger roared loudly, its sound shaking the mountains and hills, a dramatic description of the tiger's reaction.
  • 翁大惧，忽醒，乃知其梦 - The old man was greatly frightened, suddenly woke up, and realized it was a dream, indicating the realization of a dream.
  • 心异之 - He found it strange, expressing the old man's reaction to the dream.
  • 遣人招丁，丁辞不至 - He sent someone to summon Ding, but Ding refused to come, indicating Ding's reluctance to visit.
  • 函戒哀切 - The letter contained earnest and sorrowful warnings, describing the tone of the father's letter.
  • 见兄门齿尽脱 - He saw that his brother's front teeth were all gone, a description of the physical state of Ding.
  • 骇而问之 - He was shocked and asked him, indicating the old man's reaction upon seeing Ding's condition.
  • 醉中坠马所折 - It was broken when he fell off a horse while drunk, explaining the cause of Ding's tooth loss.
  • 益骇 - He was even more shocked, indicating a heightened sense of surprise.
  • 间曰 - He asked cautiously, indicating a careful and measured inquiry.
  • 此幻梦之适符耳，何足怪 - This is just a coincidental match of a dream, nothing to be surprised about, a dismissive response to the old man's concerns.
  • 时方赂当路者，得首荐 - At the time, he was bribing influential officials to get the top recommendation, indicating Ding's corrupt practices.
  • 故不以妖梦为意 - Therefore, he did not take the dream seriously, showing Ding's disregard for the dream.
  • 弟居数日，见其蠹役满堂，纳贿关说者中夜不绝 - After staying for several days, he saw that the hall was filled with corrupt officials and people bribing and pleading all night long, describing the corrupt environment.
  • 流涕谏止之 - He wept and tried to dissuade him, indicating the younger brother's emotional and moral appeal.
  • 黜陟之权，在上台不在百姓 - The power to promote or demote lies with the higher authorities, not with the common people, a cynical view of political power.
  • 上台喜，便是好官；爱百姓，何术能令上台喜也 - If the higher authorities are pleased, then he is a good official; how can loving the common people make the higher authorities pleased? A pragmatic and cynical view of governance.
  • 弟知不可劝止，遂归告父 - Knowing that he could not dissuade him, the younger brother returned home to report to his father, indicating the futility of the younger brother's efforts.
  • 翁闻之大哭 - The old man heard this and wept bitterly, showing his emotional distress.
  • 无可如何，惟捐家济贫，日祷于神 - Feeling helpless, he only donated his family's wealth to help the poor and prayed to the gods daily, indicating the old man's desperate actions.
  • 但求逆子之报，不累妻孥 - He only asked for retribution against the wayward son, without involving his wife and children, showing the old man's focused anger.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金甲猛士 - Golden-armored warriors, likely supernatural beings or spirits.
  • 黑索 - Black ropes, used in the context of binding and transforming the armor into a tiger.
  • 虎 - Tiger, a symbol of power and ferocity in Chinese culture.
  • 利剑 - Sharp sword, a weapon used in the context of killing the tiger.
  • 巨锤 - Large hammer, used to knock out the tiger's teeth.
  • 山岳 - Mountains and hills, used metaphorically to describe the intensity of the tiger's roar.
  • 函戒 - Letter containing warnings, a formal and serious communication.
  • 蠹役 - Corrupt officials, a term used to describe those who abuse their power.
  • 纳贿关说 - Bribery and pleading, practices used to influence officials.
  • 黜陟 - Promotion or demotion, terms used in the context of political appointments.
  • 上台 - Higher authorities, referring to influential officials or the government.
  • 百姓 - Common people, the general population.
  • 捐家济贫 - Donating family wealth to help the poor, an act of charity.
  • 逆子 - Wayward son, a term used to describe a disobedient or ungrateful child.
  • 妻孥 - Wife and children, a term used to refer to the family.

Proper Nouns

  • 翁 - The old man, the narrator of the story.
  • 丁 - Ding, the younger brother.
  • 甲 - The elder brother, Ding's brother.
  • 次子 - The younger brother, Ding.
  • 父 - The father, the old man's father.
  • 弟 - The younger brother, Ding.
  • 衡茅 - A humble dwelling, a term used to describe a simple life.
  • 上台 - Higher authorities, referring to influential officials or the government.
  • 百姓 - Common people, the general population.
  • 妻孥 - Wife and children, a term used to refer to the family.

[1047]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 次年 - Means 'the following year,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 贺者盈门 - Literally 'congratulators fill the door,' a common expression for a house full of visitors congratulating someone.
  • 欷歔 - Means 'sighing' or 'muttering,' often used to express sorrow or frustration.
  • 伏枕托疾不出 - Means 'lying down pretending to be ill and not going out,' a euphemism for staying in seclusion due to grief or illness.
  • 未几 - Means 'shortly after' or 'not long afterward,' indicating a brief period of time.
  • 子归途遇寇 - Means 'the son met bandits on his way back home,' with '子' referring to Jia's son.
  • 主仆殒命 - Means 'master and servant died,' indicating that both Jia's son and his attendant were killed.
  • 止及其身 - Means 'only affecting his person,' suggesting that the punishment or consequence did not extend beyond the individual.
  • 祐我家者不可谓不厚也 - Means 'those who protect our family cannot be said to have been unkind,' expressing gratitude for divine protection.
  • 因焚香而报谢之 - Means 'therefore, burning incense to offer thanks,' a ritual act of gratitude.
  • 咸以为道路讹传 - Means 'everyone thought it was a rumor from the road,' indicating disbelief in the reported news.
  • 刻日为之营兆 - Means 'making plans to arrange the burial site,' with '刻日' emphasizing the urgency and '营兆' referring to preparing the grave.
  • 而甲固未死 - Means 'but Jia had not actually died,' revealing that the reported death was false.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 荐举 - Refers to the practice of recommending someone for an official position, a common method of selection in ancient China.
  • 吏部 - The Ministry of Personnel, one of the six ministries in the imperial bureaucracy responsible for appointments and promotions.
  • 寇 - Refers to bandits or robbers, a common threat in ancient times.
  • 道路讹传 - Refers to rumors spread along the roads, a common source of misinformation in ancient times.

Proper Nouns

  • 甲 - The name of the person being discussed, likely a common name but specific to this context.
  • 翁 - Refers to the old man, likely Jia's father or another elderly relative.
  • 子 - Refers to Jia's son, the son of the person being discussed.

[1048]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先是 - Temporal phrase meaning 'before this' or 'previously'.
  • 四月間 - Refers to the month of April, with '間' indicating a period or time frame.
  • 解任 - Means 'to resign from office'.
  • 甫離境 - Means 'just left the territory' or 'as soon as he left the area'.
  • 遭寇 - Means 'encountered bandits' or 'was attacked by robbers'.
  • 傾裝以獻之 - Means 'emptied his belongings to offer to them'.
  • 一邑之民 - Refers to 'the people of one county' or 'the inhabitants of a single district'.
  • 泄冤愤 - Means 'to vent grievances' or 'to express resentment'.
  • 寧專為此哉 - Means 'would we do this solely for this purpose?' or 'would we do it just for this?'
  • 決其首 - Means 'cut off his head'.
  • 有司大成者誰是 - Means 'who among the officials is the most powerful?'
  • 腹心 - Refers to 'close confidants' or 'intimate associates'.
  • 助纣为虐 - Means 'to assist the wicked in their evil deeds' or 'to help the tyrant in his tyranny'.
  • 蠹役 - Refers to 'corrupt officials' or 'corrupt clerks'.
  • 聚敛臣 - Means 'a minister who accumulates wealth'.
  • 携入都 - Means 'took them to the capital'.
  • 并搜决讫 - Means 'searched and executed them all'.
  • 分资入囊 - Means 'divided the loot into their bags'.
  • 骛驰而去 - Means 'galloped away' or 'fled quickly'.
  • 魂伏道旁 - Means 'his soul lay by the roadside'.
  • 宰官 - Refers to 'a high-ranking official' or 'a governor'.
  • 白知县 - Refers to 'Mr. Bai, the county magistrate'.
  • 邪人不宜使正 - Means 'a wicked person should not be treated as a virtuous one'.
  • 以肩承领可也 - Means 'let him carry it on his shoulder instead'.
  • 移时复苏 - Means 'after some time, he revived'.
  • 妻子往收其尸 - Means 'his wife and children went to retrieve his body'.
  • 有余息 - Means 'still breathing' or 'with a faint breath'.
  • 寄旅邸 - Means 'stayed in a temporary lodging'.
  • 贫不能归 - Means 'poor and unable to return home'.
  • 半年许 - Means 'about half a year'.
  • 翁始得确耗 - Means 'the old man finally received definite news'.
  • 遣次子致之而归 - Means 'sent his second son to bring him back'.
  • 目能自顾其背 - Means 'he could see his own back'.
  • 不复齿人数矣 - Means 'he was no longer counted among the living'.
  • 政声 - Refers to 'a good reputation in governance'.
  • 行取为御史 - Means 'was selected to serve as a censor'.
  • 悉符所梦 - Means 'fully matched what was dreamed'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 四月间 - The month of April, which is significant in the context of the narrative.
  • 知县 - Refers to a county magistrate, a local government official during the Qing dynasty.
  • 御史 - Refers to a censor, a high-ranking official responsible for monitoring the conduct of other officials.
  • 聚敛臣 - Refers to officials who were known for accumulating wealth, often through corrupt means.
  • 行取 - A term used in the imperial examination system, indicating that someone was selected to serve in a higher position.
  • 悉符所梦 - Refers to a dream that came true, suggesting a supernatural element or divine intervention.

Proper Nouns

  • 甲 - The protagonist, a county magistrate.
  • 白知县 - Mr. Bai, the county magistrate.
  • 翁 - The old man, likely Jia's father or a close relative.
  • 翁姊子 - The old man's sister's son, who becomes a censor.
  • 御史 - A censor, a high-ranking official.

[1049]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 官虎而吏狼 - A metaphor comparing corrupt officials to tigers and corrupt clerks to wolves, highlighting the pervasive nature of corruption.
  • 比比也 - Means 'everywhere' or 'everywhere you look,' emphasizing the ubiquity of corruption.
  • 即官不为虎，而吏且将为狼 - A conditional statement suggesting that even if officials are not corrupt, clerks will still be corrupt.
  • 况有猛于虎者耶 - A rhetorical question implying the existence of even more dangerous corrupt officials than tigers.
  • 夫人患不能自顾其后耳 - Means 'people are afraid they can't look after themselves,' expressing the fear of corruption.
  • 苏而使之自顾，鬼神之教微矣哉 - Means 'if they are awakened and made to look after themselves, the influence of ghosts and gods will diminish,' suggesting that self-reliance can reduce the need for supernatural intervention.
  • 居官颇廉明 - Means 'the magistrate was quite upright and clear,' describing the magistrate's character.
  • 门役吓之曰 - Means 'the doorkeeper threatened him, saying,' introducing the corrupt behavior of the doorkeeper.
  • 官索汝二百金，宜速办；不然，败矣 - Means 'the official demands two hundred gold coins from you, should be quickly prepared; otherwise, you will be ruined,' a typical extortion demand.
  • 役摇手不可，富民苦哀之 - Means 'the doorkeeper shook his head, saying it was not enough, and the wealthy man pleaded with him,' showing the corrupt behavior of the doorkeeper.
  • 待听鞫时，汝目睹我为若白之，其允与否，亦可明我意之无他也 - Means 'wait until the trial, you will see me plead for you, whether he agrees or not, it will also show that I have no other intention,' a manipulative tactic by the doorkeeper.
  • 适言其数，官摇首不许，汝见之耶 - Means 'just now, I mentioned the amount, the official shook his head, refusing, did you see it?' another manipulative tactic.
  • 适首肯，汝见之耶 - Means 'just now, he nodded, did you see it?' another manipulative tactic.
  • 既而审结，富民果获免，役即收其苞苴，且索谢金 - Means 'after the trial, the wealthy man was indeed acquitted, and the doorkeeper immediately took his bribe and demanded a gratuity,' describing the corrupt outcome.
  • 官自以为廉，而骂其贪者载道焉 - Means 'the official thought he was clean, but those who called him greedy were everywhere,' highlighting the discrepancy between the official's self-perception and reality.
  • 此又纵狼而不自知者矣 - Means 'this is another case of letting the wolves run wild without realizing it,' criticizing the official's ignorance of the corruption.
  • 世之如此类者更多，可为居官者备一鉴也 - Means 'there are many more like this in the world, it can serve as a mirror for officials,' suggesting that such cases are common and should be a warning.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 异史氏曰 - Refers to the author of the story, likely a pseudonym or a reference to a historical figure.
  • 李进士匡九 - Refers to a magistrate named Li Jinshi Kuangjiu, a historical or fictional figure.
  • 门役 - Refers to a doorkeeper or servant, a common figure in Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 听鞫 - Refers to a trial or hearing, a legal procedure in ancient China.
  • 苞苴 - Refers to a bribe or gift, a common form of corruption.
  • 谢金 - Refers to a gratuity or tip, another form of corruption.

Proper Nouns

  • 异史氏 - The author of the story, likely a pseudonym or a reference to a historical figure.
  • 李进士匡九 - A magistrate named Li Jinshi Kuangjiu, a historical or fictional figure.
  • 门役 - A doorkeeper or servant, a common figure in Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 听鞫 - A trial or hearing, a legal procedure in ancient China.
  • 苞苴 - A bribe or gift, a common form of corruption.
  • 谢金 - A gratuity or tip, another form of corruption.

[1050]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又 - Conjunction meaning 'furthermore' or 'also,' used to introduce additional information.
  • 性刚鲠 - Describes a person as having a straightforward and uncompromising personality.
  • 撄其怒者必死 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'those who provoke his anger will surely die,' emphasizing his extreme severity.
  • 尤恶隶皂 - Means 'especially hates servants and lower officials,' indicating a strong aversion.
  • 小过不宥 - Means 'does not forgive minor mistakes,' showing his strictness.
  • 凛坐堂上 - Describes a person sitting on the hall in a stern and intimidating manner.
  • 胥吏之属无敢咳者 - Means 'no one among the clerks and officials dares to cough,' highlighting his intimidating presence.
  • 此属间有所白，必反而用之 - Means 'if they dare to report something, he will use it against them,' indicating his cunning and manipulative nature.
  • 邑人犯重罪，惧死 - Means 'a townsman committed a serious crime and was afraid of death,' setting up the main conflict.
  • 一吏索重赂，为之缓颊 - Means 'an official demanded a large bribe to plead on his behalf,' introducing the bribe-taker.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Phrases like '性刚鲠，撄其怒者必死' introduce the magistrate's character and his extreme severity, which must be read together. Similarly, '尤恶隶皂，小过不宥' describes the magistrate's specific dislikes and his strictness, which must be read together. Other examples include '凛坐堂上，胥吏之属无敢咳者' describing the magistrate's intimidating presence, and '此属间有所白，必反而用之' describing his cunning and manipulative nature. The main conflict is set up by '邑人犯重罪，惧死,' and the bribe-taker is introduced by '一吏索重赂，为之缓颊.' The threat from the official is '不速实供，大人械梏死矣,' and the rhetorical question from the magistrate is '何知我必械梏之耶.' The sarcastic remark from the magistrate is '想其赂未到耳,' and the outcome is shown by '遂责吏，释邑人.' The warning from the townsman is '要知狼诈多端，此辈败我阴，甚至丧我身家,' and the sarcastic comment from the townsman is '不知居官者作何心腑，偏要以赤子饲麻胡也.' The concluding phrase '夜明' indicates the end of the story or a reflective moment.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 忽欲歸寧，要黃御送之 - This sentence introduces a sudden desire and the invitation for escort, which must be read together.
  • 向言無家，何前後之舛 - The question about inconsistency requires understanding the context of previous statements.
  • 曩漫言之 - The casual nature of the previous statement adds nuance to the narrative.
  • 妾家頗裕，君竭資而往，必無相虧 - This conditional statement must be read as a promise of no financial loss.
  • 要商子過，驚其絕，反舟綴之，而黃不知也 - The sequence of events involving the merchant's son must be read as a continuous action.
  • 妾相從數年，未能為君育男女，亦一不了事 - The statement about the inability to have children adds emotional depth to the narrative.
  • 妾雖陋，幸未老耄，有能以千金相贈者，便鬻妾去，此中妻室、田廬皆備焉 - The offer to sell herself must be read as a desperate measure.
  • 此計如何 - The question about the plan must be read as a moment of hesitation and uncertainty.
  • 失色 - The reaction of the male character must be read as a moment of shock or disbelief.
  • 天下固多佳人，誰肯以千金買妾者 - The rhetorical question about the rarity of such offers must be read as a reflection on the situation.
  • 戲言於外，以覘其有無 - The plan to test the waters must be read as a strategic move.
  • 賣不賣，固自在君耳 - The final decision must be read as a matter of personal choice.

[1098]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 数日榜放 - Refers to the announcement of the results after a few days, where '榜放' is a term for the public posting of examination results.
  • 生竟领荐 - Means the candidate (生) ultimately received a recommendation, with '领荐' indicating acceptance of a nomination.
  • 王下第 - Indicates that Wang failed the examination, with '下第' being a term for failing an imperial examination.
  • 走告僧 - Means to go and inform the monk, with '走告' indicating a hurried or urgent notification.
  • 盲于目 - Literally 'blind in the eyes,' used metaphorically to suggest literal blindness.
  • 不盲于鼻 - Literally 'not blind in the nose,' used metaphorically to suggest keen perception, especially in discerning the quality of writing.
  • 帘中人并鼻盲矣 - A sarcastic remark suggesting that the examiners are so incompetent they cannot even smell (metaphorically, discern) good writing.
  • 啖人水角 - A metaphorical expression meaning to consume or exploit someone's efforts, possibly referring to the examiners taking advantage of the candidates' hard work.
  • 剜我盲瞳去 - A hyperbolic threat, literally 'pluck out my blind eyes,' used to emphasize the monk's frustration and determination.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 数日榜放 - Refers to the traditional practice of announcing the results of the imperial examinations, which were crucial for selecting officials in ancient China.
  • 生竟领荐 - Indicates success in the examination, which was a significant achievement for scholars.
  • 王下第 - Failure in the examination, which could have serious implications for one's career prospects.
  • 走告僧 - The act of informing the monk, who is likely a respected figure with knowledge of the examination system.
  • 盲于目 - A reference to the monk's physical blindness, but also a metaphor for his keen insight into the quality of writing.
  • 帘中人并鼻盲矣 - A critique of the examiners, suggesting their incompetence in evaluating the candidates' work.
  • 啖人水角 - A metaphorical expression that reflects the competitive and often cutthroat nature of the examination system.
  • 剜我盲瞳去 - A dramatic and hyperbolic threat, emphasizing the monk's strong reaction to the situation.

Proper Nouns

  • 生 - Refers to the candidate who passed the examination.
  • 王 - Refers to the candidate who failed the examination.
  • 僧 - Refers to the monk, who is a respected figure with knowledge of the examination system.
  • 余杭生 - Refers to a new character who arrives on the scene, likely another candidate or a scholar.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初不知而骤嗅之 - Initially unaware but suddenly smelling it; describes a sudden realization or reaction.
  • 刺于鼻，棘于腹 - Pierces the nose, pricks the abdomen; metaphorical description of an intense and uncomfortable sensation.
  • 膀胱所不能容 - Too intense for the bladder to contain; hyperbolic description of something overwhelming or unbearable.
  • 直自下部出矣 - Directly comes out from below; euphemistic description of the release of something uncomfortable or unpleasant.
  • 去，曰：“明日自见！勿悔！勿悔！” - Left, saying, 'I will see you tomorrow! Do not regret it! Do not regret it!'; repetition of '勿悔' emphasizes determination and resolve.
  • 越二二日竟不至 - After two days, he did not come; '越二二日' is ambiguous, could mean 'after two days' or 'after two days and two nights'.
  • 视之已移去矣 - Seeing that he had already moved away; '视之已移去矣' is ambiguous, could mean 'seeing that he had already left' or 'seeing that he had already moved away'.
  • 乃知即某门生也 - Then it was known that he was a certain disciple; '乃知' indicates a realization or understanding.
  • 凡吾辈读书人，不当尤人，但当克己 - For all of us who study, we should not blame others, but should control ourselves; '尤人' (blame others) and '克己' (control oneself) are key moral principles in Confucian thought.
  • 不尤人则德益弘，能克己则学益进 - Not blaming others makes virtue more expansive, controlling oneself makes learning more advanced; describes the benefits of self-control and non-blame.
  • 当前踧落，固是数之不偶 - Currently being overlooked, it is indeed a matter of bad luck; '踧落' (being overlooked) and '数之不偶' (bad luck) describe a situation where someone is not recognized or appreciated.
  • 平心而论，文亦未便登峰 - Objectively speaking, the writing is not yet at the top; '平心而论' (objectively speaking) and '文亦未便登峰' (the writing is not yet at the top) describe a realistic assessment of one's abilities.
  • 其由此砥砺，天下自有不盲之人 - By refining oneself from here, there will be people in the world who are not blind; '砥砺' (refining oneself) and '天下自有不盲之人' (there will be people in the world who are not blind) describe the potential for improvement and recognition.
  • 王肃然起敬 - Wang respectfully stood up; '肃然起敬' (respectfully stood up) describes a formal and respectful gesture.
  • 薪桂米珠 - Firewood as expensive as cassia, rice as precious as pearls; hyperbolic description of the high cost of living in the capital.
  • 勿忧资斧 - Do not worry about expenses; '资斧' (expenses) is archaic, could mean 'money' or 'resources'.
  • 舍后有窖镪 - Behind the house there is a buried treasure; '窖镪' (buried treasure) describes a hidden stash of money.
  • 可以发用 - Can be used; '可以发用' (can be used) describes the practicality of the treasure.
  • 昔窦、范贫而能廉 - In the past, Dou and Fan were poor but could be廉洁 (honest); '贫而能廉' (poor but honest) describes a virtuous quality.
  • 今某幸能自给，敢自污乎 - Now I am fortunate to be self-sufficient, how dare I defile myself?; '敢自污乎' (how dare I defile myself) expresses a sense of honor and integrity.
  • 一日醉眠 - One day, he fell asleep drunk; '醉眠' (fell asleep drunk) describes a state of intoxication.
  • 仆及庖人窃发之 - The servant and cook secretly opened it; '窃发之' (secretly opened it) describes a covert action.
  • 情见事露 - The truth was revealed; '情见事露' (the truth was revealed) describes a situation where a secret is uncovered.
  • 方诃责间 - Just as he was scolding them; '方诃责间' (just as he was scolding them) describes a moment of confrontation.
  • 见有金爵 - Seeing golden cups; '金爵' (golden cups) describes valuable items.
  • 类多镌款 - Mostly engraved with inscriptions; '镌款' (engraved with inscriptions) describes the markings on the items.
  • 审视皆大父字讳 - Upon closer inspection, they were all inscribed with the name of my grandfather; '大父字讳' (the name of my grandfather) describes a personal connection to the items.

  • 然命薄不足任仕进也 - However, your fate is not suitable for a career in public service.

  • 后以故诣金陵，遇余杭生于旅次，极道契阔，深自降抑，然鬓毛斑矣 - Later, you visited Nanjing and met a person from Yuhang on a journey, discussing deep friendship and showing humility despite your gray hair.
  • 异史氏曰 - The historian of strange tales said.
  • 天人之厌弃已久，故鬼神皆玩弄之 - Heaven and man have long abandoned him, so spirits play with him.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王 - Refers to a king or ruler.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孙大怒，挞儿 - Reflects a traditional family structure and the authority of elders.
  • 妇苏，力止之，且喜曰 - Shows the dynamics of a traditional Chinese family, where the wife plays a mediating role.
  • 妾昔虐儿，中心每不自释 - Reflects the moral and ethical values of the time regarding guilt over mistreating a child.
  • 今幸销一罪案矣 - Common theme in classical Chinese literature about atonement for past wrongs.
  • 孙益嬖爱之，妇每拒，使就妾宿 - Reflects the complex social dynamics of the time involving relationships between grandfather, wife, and concubine.
  • 居数年，屡产屡殇 - Reflects the harsh realities of life in traditional Chinese society with repeated loss.
  • 此昔日杀儿之报也 - Common theme in classical Chinese literature about karmic retribution.
  • 阿坚既娶，遂以外事委儿，内事委媳 - Reflects the traditional division of labor in a family.
  • 一日曰：“妾某日当死。”孙不信 - Reflects the supernatural elements often found in classical Chinese literature with predictions of death.
  • 妇自理葬具，至日更衣入棺而卒 - Reflects the importance of funeral rites in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 颜色如生，异香满室；既殓，香始渐灭 - Reflects the belief in the afterlife and the presence of spirits.
  • 异史氏曰 - Common device in classical Chinese literature to introduce a moral or philosophical reflection.
  • 心之所好，原不在妍媸也 - Reflects the influence of Confucian and Daoist thought on the nature of desire.
  • 毛嫱、西施，焉知非自爱之者美之乎？ - Reflects the cultural significance of beauty and self-love.
  • 然不遭悍妒，其贤不彰 - Reflects the moral and ethical values of the time regarding jealousy.
  • 几令人与嗜痂者并笑矣 - Reflects the cultural significance of unusual or perverse tastes.
  • 至锦屏之人，其夙根原厚，故豁然一悟，立证菩提 - Reflects the influence of Buddhist thought on spiritual awakening.
  • 若地狱道中，皆富贵而不经艰难者矣 - Reflects a critique of those who have not faced adversity.

[1119]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 商恪遵治命 - 商 (Shang) refers to a person, and 恪遵治命 (ke zun zhi ming) means 'strictly obeying the will of the deceased.' This phrase indicates that 商 is adhering to the wishes of someone who has passed away.
  • 誠朴無二 - This phrase means 'sincere and simple without deceit,' describing 商's character.
  • 躬耕自给 - 躬耕 (gong geng) means 'tilling the land personally,' and 自给 (zi ji) means 'self-sufficient.' Together, this phrase describes 商's lifestyle of working the land to sustain himself.
  • 乡人咸爱敬之 - 乡人 (xiang ren) means 'villagers,' 咸 (xian) means 'all,' 爱敬 (ai jing) means 'love and respect,' and 之 (zhi) is a pronoun referring to 商. This phrase indicates that all the villagers love and respect 商.
  • 假以资使学负贩 - 假 (jia) means 'to lend,' 资 (zi) means 'money or capital,' 使 (shi) means 'to make,' 学 (xue) means 'to learn,' and 负贩 (fu fan) means 'to carry goods for sale.' This phrase means that a wealthy man lent 商 money to learn how to carry goods for sale.
  • 辄亏其母 - 辄 (zhe) means 'always,' 亏 (kui) means 'to harm or damage,' and 其母 (qi mu) means 'his mother.' This phrase indicates that 商 always harmed his mother's interests.
  • 愧无以偿 - 愧 (kui) means 'ashamed,' 无以 (wu yi) means 'unable to,' and 偿 (chang) means 'to repay.' This phrase means that 商 felt ashamed because he could not repay the debt.
  • 请为佣 - 请 (qing) means 'to request,' 为 (wei) means 'to be,' and 佣 (yong) means 'a hired worker.' This phrase means that 商 requested to become a hired worker.
  • 翁不肯 - 翁 (weng) means 'old man' or 'elderly man,' and 不肯 (bu ken) means 'refused.' This phrase indicates that the elderly man refused 商's request.
  • 商瞿然不自安 - 商 (Shang) is the name of the person, 瞿然 (qu ran) means 'suddenly or startled,' and 不自安 (bu zi an) means 'feeling uneasy.' This phrase describes 商's reaction to the situation.
  • 尽货其田宅 - 尽 (jin) means 'to sell everything,' 货 (huo) means 'to sell,' 其 (qi) is a pronoun referring to 商's, 田宅 (tian zhai) means 'land and houses.' This phrase means that 商 sold all his land and houses.
  • 往酬翁 - 往 (wang) means 'to go,' 酬 (chou) means 'to repay,' and 翁 (weng) means 'elderly man.' This phrase means that 商 went to repay the elderly man.
  • 翁请得情 - 翁 (weng) means 'elderly man,' 请 (qing) means 'to ask,' 得 (de) means 'to understand,' and 情 (qing) means 'the situation.' This phrase means that the elderly man understood the situation.
  • 益直之 - 益 (yi) means 'more,' 直 (zhi) means 'to increase,' and 之 (zhi) is a pronoun referring to the repayment. This phrase means that the elderly man increased the repayment.
  • 强为赎还旧业 - 强 (qiang) means 'to force,' 为 (wei) means 'to do,' 赎还 (shu huan) means 'to redeem,' and 旧业 (jiu ye) means 'old business or property.' This phrase means that the elderly man forced 商 to redeem his old property.
  • 又益贷以重金 - 又 (you) means 'again,' 益 (yi) means 'more,' 贷 (dai) means 'to lend,' 以 (yi) means 'with,' and 重金 (zhong jin) means 'a large sum of money.' This phrase means that the elderly man lent 商 a large sum of money again.
  • 俾作贾 - 俾 (bi) means 'to make,' 作 (zuo) means 'to do,' and 贾 (gua) means 'merchant.' This phrase means that the elderly man made 商 become a merchant.
  • 十数金尚不能偿 - 十数金 (shi shu jin) means 'a dozen or so gold coins,' 尚 (shang) means 'still,' 不能 (bu neng) means 'cannot,' and 偿 (chang) means 'to repay.' This phrase means that 商 still could not repay a dozen or so gold coins.
  • 奈何结来生驴马债耶 - 奈何 (nai he) means 'how can,' 结 (jie) means 'to tie,' 来生 (lai sheng) means 'next life,' 驴马债 (lu ma zhai) means 'debt of donkeys and horses,' and 耶 (ye) is a particle used at the end of a question. This phrase means that 商 asked how he could tie up a debt of donkeys and horses in his next life.
  • 再强之 - 再 (zai) means 'again,' 强 (qiang) means 'to force,' and 之 (zhi) is a pronoun referring to 商. This phrase means that the elderly man tried to force 商 again.
  • 必不可 - 必 (bi) means 'certainly,' 不 (bu) means 'not,' and 可 (ke) means 'can.' This phrase means that 商 certainly could not do it.
  • 躬耕如故 - 躬耕 (gong geng) means 'tilling the land personally,' and 如故 (ru gu) means 'as before.' This phrase means that 商 tilled the land as before.
  • 每自叹曰 - 每 (mei) means 'every,' 自 (zi) means 'himself,' 叹 (tan) means 'to sigh,' and 曰 (yue) means 'said.' This phrase means that 商 often sighed and said.
  • 人生世上，皆有数年之享 - 人生世上 (ren sheng shi shang) means 'life on earth,' 皆 (jie) means 'all,' 有 (you) means 'have,' 数年 (shu nian) means 'a few years,' and 享 (xiang) means 'enjoyment.' This phrase means that all people have a few years of enjoyment.
  • 何遂落魄如此 - 何遂 (he sui) means 'why did,' 落魄 (luo po) means 'to be destitute,' and 如此 (ru ci) means 'like this.' This phrase means that 商 wondered why he was so destitute.
  • 会有外来巫 - 会 (hui) means 'there happened to be,' 有 (you) means 'have,' 外来 (wai lai) means 'from outside,' and 巫 (wu) means 'a fortune-teller.' This phrase means that there happened to be a fortune-teller from outside.
  • 以钱卜 - 以 (yi) means 'with,' 钱 (qian) means 'money,' and 卜 (bu) means 'to divinate.' This phrase means that the fortune-teller used money to divinate.
  • 悉知人运数 - 悉 (xi) means 'all,' 知 (zhi) means 'to know,' 人 (ren) means 'people,' and 运数 (yun shu) means 'destiny.' This phrase means that the fortune-teller knew all about people's destinies.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 商 - A person's name, likely a commoner.
  • 翁 - Refers to an elderly man, often used to show respect.
  • 田宅 - Land and houses, important assets in traditional Chinese society.
  • 负贩 - Carrying goods for sale, a common occupation for the lower class.
  • 佣 - A hired worker, often used to describe someone who works for wages.
  • 贾 - Merchant, a person who buys and sells goods.
  • 驴马债 - A metaphorical expression for a heavy debt, often used to describe a significant financial burden.
  • 巫 - Fortune-teller, a person who practices divination and other spiritual practices.
  • 钱卜 - Divination using money, a practice that was common in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 运数 - Destiny, a concept that suggests that one's fate is predetermined.

[1120]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 敬诣之 - Respectful way of saying 'to visit' or 'to go to'; indicates the protagonist's respectful approach.
  • 寓室精洁 - Describes the room as being clean and tidy; emphasizes the witch's attention to detail.
  • 中设神座 - Refers to the central placement of a shrine or altar; suggests a sacred space.
  • 香气常熏 - Indicates a persistent fragrance; implies a sense of reverence or sanctity.
  • 商人朝拜讫 - Means 'the merchant has finished his morning worship'; shows a ritualistic aspect.
  • 巫便索资 - The witch then asks for payment; indicates a transactional element.
  • 商授百钱 - The merchant gives the witch a hundred coins; specifies the amount.
  • 巫尽纳木筒中 - The witch places all the money in a wooden tube; suggests a ritual or symbolic act.
  • 执跪座下 - The witch kneels in front of the shrine; indicates a submissive or reverent posture.
  • 摇响如祈签状 - The witch shakes something to produce a sound, similar to drawing a lot; suggests a divination ritual.
  • 已而起 - Afterward, she stands up; marks the end of the ritual.
  • 倾钱入手 - She pours the money into her hand; indicates a transfer of ownership.
  • 而后于案上次第摆之 - Then she arranges the coins on the table in a specific order; suggests a methodical or ritualistic process.
  • 其法以字为否，幕为亨 - The method is to interpret the arrangement of the coins, where '字' (heads) means '否' (no) and '幕' (tails) means '亨' (yes).
  • 数至五十八皆字，以后则尽幕矣 - Up to the 58th coin, all are heads, after which they are all tails; indicates a pattern or prophecy.
  • 遂问 - The merchant then asks a question; initiates a dialogue.
  • 庚甲几何 - This phrase is a bit ambiguous; could mean 'What is the year?' or 'How many years?' depending on the context.
  • 早矣 - Means 'too early' or 'too soon'; indicates a sense of urgency or disappointment.
  • 官人现行者先人运，非身运 - The merchant's current luck is that of his ancestors, not his own; suggests a generational influence.
  • 五十八岁方交本身运 - At the age of 58, he will experience his own luck; indicates a turning point.
  • 始无盘错也 - He will then be free from difficulties; suggests a resolution of problems.
  • 何谓先人运 - The merchant asks what '先人运' means; seeks clarification.
  • 先人有善，其福未尽，则后人享之；先人有不善，其祸未尽，则后人亦受之 - Explains that the good or bad deeds of ancestors have lingering effects on their descendants; indicates a belief in generational karma.
  • 屈指曰 - The merchant counts on his fingers; indicates a calculation or reflection.
  • 再三十年，齿已老耆，行就木矣 - In another thirty years, he will be old and near death; suggests a sense of mortality.
  • 五十八以前，便有回国，略可营谋 - Before the age of 58, he will return home and can make some plans; indicates a period of preparation.
  • 然仅免饥寒耳 - However, he will only avoid hunger and cold; suggests limited success.
  • 五十八之年，当有巨金自来，不须力求 - At the age of 58, a large sum of money will come to him without effort; indicates a stroke of luck.
  • 官人生无过行，再世享之不尽也 - The merchant's good deeds will bring him endless benefits in future lives; suggests a belief in reincarnation and karma.
  • 疑信半焉 - The merchant is half doubtful and half believing; indicates a state of uncertainty.
  • 安贫自守，不敢妄求 - He remains poor but content, not daring to seek more; suggests a sense of resignation or contentment.
  • 后至五十三岁，留意验之 - After reaching 53, he pays attention to verifying the prophecy; indicates a turning point in his attitude.
  • 时方东作，病痁不能耕 - At the time, he was engaged in spring farming but fell ill with malaria and could not work; suggests a period of hardship.
  • 既痊，天大旱，早禾尽枯 - After recovering, there was a severe drought, and the early rice crops all withered; indicates a natural disaster.
  • 近秋方雨，家无别种，田数亩悉以种谷 - Just before autumn, it rained, but his family had no other seeds, so he planted the few acres of land with rice; suggests a desperate attempt to salvage the harvest.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 巫 - Refers to a witch or shaman; indicates a figure with spiritual or supernatural powers.
  • 神座 - Refers to a shrine or altar; suggests a sacred space.
  • 百钱 - Refers to a hundred coins; indicates a specific amount of money.
  • 木筒 - Refers to a wooden tube; suggests a container for money or other items.
  • 祈签 - Refers to a form of divination; indicates a method of seeking guidance.
  • 字 - Refers to the heads of the coins; indicates a positive outcome.
  • 幕 - Refers to the tails of the coins; indicates a negative outcome.
  • 庚甲 - Refers to the year or age; indicates a time-related question.
  • 先人运 - Refers to the luck of ancestors; indicates a generational influence.
  • 身运 - Refers to personal luck; indicates an individual's fate.
  • 盘错 - Refers to difficulties or entanglements; indicates a state of trouble.
  • 痁 - Refers to malaria; indicates a specific illness.
  • 东作 - Refers to spring farming; indicates a seasonal activity.
  • 早禾 - Refers to early rice crops; indicates a specific crop.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 既而又旱，葬菽半死，惟谷无恙 - This sequence of events must be read as a series of connected occurrences: 'Afterward there was a drought, half of the beans died, but only the grain remained unharmed.'
  • 后得雨勃发，其丰倍焉 - This clause describes the aftermath of the drought: 'Later, after the rain, they flourished, and their yield doubled.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 美豐標 - Describes Yao An's physical appearance, meaning 'handsome and well-proportioned'.
  • 艷而知書 - Describes Lü'e's qualities, meaning 'beautiful and well-educated'.
  • 擇偶不嫁 - Indicates Lü'e's selectivity in choosing a partner, meaning 'chose a husband but did not marry'.
  • 門族風采，必如姚某始字之 - Explains the condition set by Lü'e's mother, meaning 'the family must have a certain reputation, and only someone like Yao An would be suitable'.
  • 給妻窺井，擠墮之 - Describes Yao An's deceitful act, meaning 'gave his wife a chance to look into the well and pushed her in'.
  • 雅甚親愛 - Describes their relationship, meaning 'they were very affectionate'.
  • 然以其美也，故疑之 - Explains Yao An's suspicion, meaning 'however, because of her beauty, he suspected her'.
  • 女欲歸寧 - Indicates her desire to return home, meaning 'when Lü'e wanted to visit her parents'.
  • 則以兩肘支袍，覆翼以出 - Describes his method of following her, meaning 'he would support his sleeves with his elbows and cover them as he went out'.
  • 入輿封志 - Describes his continued surveillance, meaning 'he would seal the carriage and follow behind'.
  • 越宿促與俱歸 - Describes his insistence on her return, meaning 'the next day, he would urge her to return home with him'.
  • 女心不能善 - Indicates her dissatisfaction, meaning 'Lü'e's heart could not accept this'.
  • 忿曰 - Introduces her reaction, meaning 'she angrily said'.
  • 若有桑中約，豈瑣瑣所能止耶 - Expresses her defiance, meaning 'if there is a secret agreement, how can such petty measures stop me?'
  • 姚以故他往 - Indicates his absence, meaning 'Yao went somewhere else'.
  • 則扃女室中 - Describes his controlling behavior, meaning 'he would lock Lü'e in her room'.
  • 女益厭之 - Indicates her growing resentment, meaning 'Lü'e became even more disgusted'.
  • 俟其去，故以他鑰置門外以疑之 - Describes her attempt to trick him, meaning 'when he left, she deliberately placed another key outside the door to make him suspicious'.
  • 姚見大怒 - Describes his reaction, meaning 'Yao saw this and became very angry'.
  • 女憤言 - Introduces her response, meaning 'Lü'e angrily said'.
  • 不知 - Lü'e's response, meaning 'I don't know'.
  • 姚愈疑 - Indicates his increased suspicion, meaning 'Yao became even more suspicious'.

  • 有车马至门 - Formal way to describe a visit, often associated with official or important guests.
  • 接于莅任 - Formal expression for receiving officials, reflecting the hierarchical nature of ancient Chinese society.
  • 一言欲告，又恐阻锐进之志 - Balanced expression of concern and caution, reflecting the delicate balance between advice and encouragement.
  • 君命淹蹇，生非其时 - Poetic way to express difficulties in one's destiny, reflecting the belief in fate and the importance of timing.

  • 勿淹滞 - Concise command to move on, reflecting the urgency of the situation.
  • 今朝年、月、日、时皆良 - Superstitious belief in favorable timing, reflecting the importance of auspicious moments in ancient Chinese culture.
  • 即以舆盖送君归 - Formal way to describe a farewell, reflecting the importance of proper etiquette.
  • 仆驰马自去 - Simple statement of departure, reflecting the protagonist's resolve to continue on their own path.
  • 中心迷乱 - Poetic expression of inner turmoil, reflecting the emotional state of the protagonist.
  • 不知所嘱 - Metaphor for being unsure of what to do, reflecting the protagonist's confusion.
  • 挥涕送之 - Vivid description of a sad farewell, reflecting the emotional weight of the moment.
  • 见舆马分途 - Metaphor for parting ways, reflecting the finality of the separation.
  • 顷刻都散 - Quick and final separation, reflecting the suddenness of the event.
  • 始悔子晋北旋，未致一字，而已无及矣 - Reflection on missed opportunities, reflecting the protagonist's regret.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 三場畢 - Refers to the completion of three exams, likely part of the imperial examination system.

  • 若然 - Means 'if it is so' or 'if that is the case.'
  • 夢舆蓋止於其門 - Describes a dream where a carriage stops at the door.
  • 美少年 - Refers to a handsome young man.
  • 自車中出 - Means 'stepping out of the carriage.'
  • 登堂展拜 - Describes entering the house and bowing respectfully.
  • 答云 - Means 'answered saying.'
  • 大哥許假一舍 - Refers to borrowing a room from an elder brother, likely a metaphorical reference to a previous life or reincarnation.
  • 我先至矣 - Means 'I have arrived first.'
  • 請入拜母 - Means 'to ask to enter and pay respects to the mother.'

[1173]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 數月後 - After several months, indicating a passage of time.
  • 一見則折腰索抱 - To bend over and embrace upon seeing someone, suggesting deep affection.
  • 走去則啼不可止 - To cry uncontrollably when leaving, indicating intense emotional attachment.
  • 陶亦狎愛之 - Tao also fondled and loved him, where '狎愛' suggests a close, intimate affection.
  • 四歲離母，辄就兄眠 - At four years old, when separated from his mother, he would go to sleep with his brother, indicating a close sibling bond.
  • 兄他出，则假寐以俟其归 - When the brother went out, he would pretend to sleep, waiting for his return, showing the child's attentiveness and loyalty.
  • 兄于枕上教毛诗，诵声呢喃，夜尽四十余行 - The brother taught him *Mao Shi* (The Book of Songs) while lying on the bed, with a soft, murmuring voice, reciting over forty lines in the night, indicating a nurturing and educational relationship.
  • 以子晋遗文授之，欣然乐读，过口成诵；试之他文不能也 - He was given the writings of Zi Jin (a historical figure), and he read them with great joy, memorizing them effortlessly; when tested with other texts, he could not do so, highlighting the child's exceptional talent.
  • 八九岁眉目朗彻，宛然一子晋矣 - At eight or nine years old, his eyebrows and eyes were clear and bright, exactly like Zi Jin, emphasizing his striking resemblance to a famous figure.
  • 陶两入闱，皆不第 - Tao took the imperial examinations twice but failed both times, indicating his academic struggles.
  • 丁酉，文场事发，帘官多遭诛遣，贡举之途一肃，乃张巡环力也 - In the year Ding You, an incident in the examination hall occurred, and many officials were punished or dismissed, leading to a strict整顿 of the examination system, which was due to Zhang Xun's efforts, indicating a significant historical event.
  • 陶下科中副车，寻贡 - Tao passed the next examination as a secondary candidate and was soon recommended, indicating his eventual success.
  • 遂灰志前途，隐居教弟 - He then lost interest in his future and retired to teach his younger brother, showing a shift in his life goals.
  • 尝语人曰：“吾有此乐，翰苑不易也。” - He once said to others, 'I have this kind of happiness, which is not easy to find in the imperial library,' indicating his contentment with his current life.
  • 异史氏曰 - The historian said, introducing a commentary.
  • 余每至张夫子庙堂，瞻其须眉，凛禀有生气 - I often visit the temple of Master Zhang, and when I look at his mustache and eyebrows, they seem to have a living presence, indicating a sense of reverence.
  • 又其生平喑哑如霹雳声，矛马所至，无不大快，出人意表 - His life was as silent as thunder, and wherever he went with his spear and horse, everything was made clear and understandable, beyond what people expected, describing his character and actions.
  • 世以将军好武，遂置与绛，灌伍，宁知文昌事繁，须侯固多哉 - The world considers generals to be good at war, so they are placed among the ranks of Jiang and Guan (historical figures), but who knows that the affairs of the imperial library are complex and require many capable individuals, indicating a contrast between military and scholarly roles.
  • 呜呼！三十五年，来何暮也 - Alas! Thirty-five years have passed, and it feels like it came too late, expressing a sense of regret and reflection.

  • 陶 - Refers to Tao, a historical figure.
  • 子晋 - Refers to Zi Jin, a historical figure known for his literary talents.
  • 丁酉 - Refers to the year Ding You, a specific year in the Chinese calendar.
  • 文场 - Refers to the examination hall, where imperial examinations were held.
  • 帘官 - Refers to officials in charge of the examination hall.
  • 贡举 - Refers to the imperial examination system.
  • 张巡环 - Refers to Zhang Xun, a historical figure known for his efforts in reforming the examination system.
  • 翰苑 - Refers to the imperial library, a prestigious position in the imperial court.
  • 张夫子 - Refers to Master Zhang, a historical figure.
  • 绛，灌 - Refers to Jiang and Guan, historical figures known for their military prowess.
  • 文昌 - Refers to the imperial library, a place of learning and scholarship.

  • 得錢輒沽 - Means 'whenever he got money, he would immediately buy alcohol.'
  • 殊不以窮厄為意 - Means 'he did not at all consider his poverty and hardship.'

  • 善飲無對 - Means 'there was no one to drink with him.'
  • 聞生名 - Means 'he heard of the scholar's name.'
  • 招與飲而悅之 - Means 'he invited him to drink and became fond of him.'
  • 時共談宴 - Means 'they often talked and feasted together.'
  • 生恃其狎 - Means 'the scholar relied on their familiarity.'
  • 凡有小訟求直者 - Means 'whenever there were minor lawsuits seeking justice.'
  • 輒受薄賄 - Means 'he would accept small bribes.'
  • 為之緩颊 - Means 'to plead on their behalf.'
  • 刺史每可其請 - Means 'the prefect always granted his requests.'
  • 生習為常 - Means 'the scholar grew accustomed to this.'
  • 刺史心厭之 - Means 'the prefect grew tired of him.'
  • 一日早衙 - Means 'one day during the morning court session.'
  • 持刺登堂 - Means 'he held a petition and went to the hall.'
  • 覽之微笑 - Means 'he looked at it and smiled.'
  • 生厲聲曰 - Means 'the scholar spoke sternly, saying.'
  • 公如所請可之；不如所請否之，何笑也！ - Means 'If you agree with what I ask, grant it; if you disagree, deny it. Why are you smiling?'
  • 聞之：士可殺而不可辱 - Means 'As they say: a scholar can be killed but not humiliated.'
  • 他固不能相報，豈一笑不能報耶？ - Means 'He certainly cannot repay me, but can I not repay him with a smile?'
  • 言已大笑 - Means 'After speaking, he laughed loudly.'
  • 聲震堂壁 - Means 'his voice echoed through the hall.'
  • 何敢無禮！寧不聞滅門令尹耶！ - Means 'How dare you be so rude! Haven't you heard of the execution of the Magistrate of Chengdu?'
  • 掉臂竟下 - Means 'he waved his arm and left.'
  • 生員無門之可滅 - Means 'I, a student, have no family to be destroyed.'
  • 執之 - Means 'they arrested him.'
  • 訪其家居 - Means 'they investigated his residence.'
  • 惟携妻在城堞上住 - Means 'he lived only with his wife on the city wall.'
  • 刺史聞而釋之 - Means 'the prefect heard about this and released him.'
  • 但逐不令居城垣 - Means 'but ordered him not to live on the city wall.'
  • 朋友怜其狂 - Means 'his friends pitied his madness.'
  • 為買数尺地，购斗室焉 - Means 'they bought a small plot of land and a tiny room for him.'
  • 入而居之 - Means 'he moved in.'
  • 今而后畏今尹矣 - Means 'From now on, I will fear this prefect.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 劉學師 - Refers to a teacher named Liu, who is relaying the story.
  • 刺史 - A prefect, a high-ranking official in ancient China responsible for governing a region.
  • 滅門令尹 - Refers to a historical event where a magistrate was executed along with his entire family, emphasizing the severity of disrespecting a high-ranking official.
  • 城堞 - Refers to the parapet of a city wall, indicating a humble and precarious living situation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 異史氏曰 - Common phrase used in classical Chinese texts to introduce a commentary or reflection, often by the author.
  • 奉法守禮 - Refers to adhering to laws and propriety, emphasizing moral and legal conduct.
  • 南面者 - A metaphor for the emperor or ruler, as the emperor traditionally sat facing south in ancient China.
  • 仇之猶得而加者 - A complex phrase meaning 'even those who harbor grudges can still inflict harm,' suggesting a situation where even enemies can cause trouble.
  • 至無門可滅 - Means 'when there is no avenue left to eliminate or harm,' indicating a state of extreme vulnerability.
  • 貧賤驕人 - A phrase meaning 'to be proud despite poverty and low status,' often used to describe someone who maintains dignity and self-respect despite their circumstances.
  • 徒以有門在耳 - Means 'merely because there is still a way out,' suggesting that the ability to escape or avoid harm is what gives people the confidence to act.
  • 喋喋公堂 - Refers to 'repeatedly arguing in court,' indicating a person who is persistent and vocal in legal disputes.
  • 品斯下矣 - Means 'this person is of low character,' suggesting that the individual's behavior is morally questionable.
  • 雖然 - A transitional phrase meaning 'although' or 'even so,' used to introduce a contrasting point.
  • 其狂不可及 - Means 'his madness is unmatched,' suggesting that the person's behavior is so extreme that it cannot be surpassed.
  • 澂俗 - A term that can be interpreted as 'clarifying the customs' or 'making the customs clear,' though its exact meaning may vary depending on context.
  • 澂人多化物類 - Means 'the people of Cheng often transform into other beings,' referring to a phenomenon where people in Cheng change into different forms.
  • 出院求食 - Means 'leaving the hospital to find food,' suggesting a situation where people leave a place of care or confinement to seek sustenance.
  • 驅之即遁 - Means 'drive them away and they will flee,' describing a situation where pests or animals can be easily scared off.
  • 驟覆之 - Means 'quickly cover it,' referring to the action of quickly covering a container.
  • 瓢水灌注其中 - Means 'pouring water into it with a ladle,' describing the act of pouring water into a container.
  • 頃之盡斃 - Means 'soon they all died,' indicating a rapid and complete demise.
  • 全家暴卒 - Means 'the whole family died suddenly,' describing a tragic event where an entire family perishes.
  • 原而宥之 - Means 'pardon and forgive him,' indicating a judicial decision to show leniency and mercy.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '至邻邑，早旦经郭外' describes the official's journey to a neighboring county and his arrival early in the morning.
  • The detailed description '见二人以床舁病人，覆大被；枕上露发，发上簪凤钗一股，侧眠床上' requires careful translation to maintain the visual imagery.
  • The clause '有三四健男夹随之，时更番以手拥被，令压身底，似恐风入' describes the care provided by the attendants, emphasizing the continuous and attentive nature of their actions.
  • The sequence '少顷息肩路侧，又使二人更相为荷' describes a pause and the rotation of responsibilities among the attendants.
  • The clause '公过，遣隶回问之，云是妹子垂危，将送归夫家' describes the official's actions and the response from the attendants.
  • The sequence '公行二三里，又遣隶回，视其所入何村' describes the official's continued journey and his instructions to his subordinate.
  • The clause '隶尾之，至一村舍，两男子迎之而入，还以白公' describes the investigation and the report back to the official.
  • The dialogue '公谓其邑宰：“城中得无有劫寇否？”宰曰：“无之。”' reflects the official's concern and the magistrate's response.
  • The clause '时功令严，上下讳盗，故即被盗贼劫杀，亦隐忍而不敢言' explains the social and legal context of the time.
  • The sequence '公就馆舍，嘱家人细访之，果有富室被强寇入室，炮烙而死' describes the official's actions and the outcome of the investigation.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence of actions in '张视几上有《南华经注》，因取就枕上伏榻翻阅' must be read as a single, continuous action: Zhang looked around, saw the *Nanhua Jing* commentary on the table, took it, lay it on the pillow, and began to read it.
  • The sudden entrance of Shunhua in '忽舜华推扉入' must be read as an abrupt interruption of Zhang's reading.
  • Zhang's actions of putting down the book and searching for his hat and shoes in '张释卷，搜觅冠履' must be read as a reaction to Shunhua's entrance.
  • The woman's quick sitting and speaking in '女即榻捷坐曰' must be read as a direct response to Zhang's actions.
  • The complex sentence in '妾以君风流才士，欲以门户相托，遂犯瓜李之嫌' must be read as a single, coherent statement expressing the woman's intentions and the potential risk involved.
  • The question in '得不相遐弃否' must be read as a plea for reassurance.
  • Zhang's state of being at a loss in '张皇然不知所对' must be read as a reaction to the woman's words.
  • Zhang's response in '但云，小生家中固有妻耳' must be read as a straightforward admission of his marital status.
  • The woman's response in '此亦见君诚笃，顾亦不妨' must be read as a reassurance and a suggestion to proceed.
  • The conditional statement in '既不嫌憎，明日当烦媒妁' must be read as a plan for the future.
  • The sequence of actions in '言已欲去，张探身挽之，女亦遂留' must be read as a single, continuous interaction.
  • The woman's early morning actions in '未曙即起，以金赠张' must be read as a gesture of goodwill.
  • The woman's instructions in '君持作临眺之资，向暮宜晚来，恐旁人所窥' must be read as a plan for Zhang's future visits.
  • Zhang's actions and the routine in '张如其言，早出晏归，半年以为常' must be read as a continuation of their relationship.

Proper Nouns

  • 张 - Zhang, the male protagonist.
  • 舜华 - Shunhua, the female protagonist.
  • 南华经注 - Commentary on the *Nanhua Jing* (Zhuangzi).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 白足提篮似丐 - Describes the appearance of a scholar entering the examination hall, comparing them to a beggar.
  • 官呵隶骂似囚 - Describes the treatment of scholars by officials and servants, comparing them to prisoners.
  • 孔孔伸头，房房露脚 - Describes the crowded and confined conditions in the examination hall, with scholars sticking their heads out of every hole and showing their feet in every room.
  • 天地异色 - Refers to the change in the scholar's perception of the world, as if the sky and earth have changed color, indicating a state of confusion and disorientation.
  • 草木皆惊 - Describes the intense anxiety and anticipation felt by scholars while waiting for the results, as if even plants and trees are startled.
  • 楼阁俱成 - Metaphor for the sudden realization of success, as if the scholar has instantly built grand structures.
  • 骸骨已朽 - Metaphor for the sudden realization of failure, as if the scholar's body has already decayed.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening phrase '秀才入闱，有七似焉' introduces a list of seven comparisons, each describing a different aspect of the examination experience.
  • The first comparison '初入时，白足提篮似丐' describes the scholar's appearance upon entering the examination hall.
  • The second comparison '唱名时，官呵隶骂似囚' describes the treatment of scholars by officials and servants.
  • The third comparison '其归号舍也，孔孔伸头，房房露脚，似秋末之冷蜂' describes the crowded and confined conditions in the examination hall.
  • The fourth comparison '其出场也，神情惝怳，天地异色，似出笼之病鸟' describes the scholar's state after leaving the examination hall.
  • The fifth comparison '迨望报也，草木皆惊，梦想亦幻' describes the intense anxiety and anticipation while waiting for the results.
  • The sixth comparison '作一得志想，则顷刻而楼阁俱成；作一失志想，则瞬息而骸骨已朽' describes the rapid and dramatic changes in the scholar's perception of success and failure.
  • The seventh comparison '此际行坐难安，则似被絷之猱' describes the restlessness and anxiety during the waiting period.
  • The final sequence '忽然而飞骑传人，报条无我，此时神色猝变，嗒然若死' describes the sudden and devastating news of failure, leading to a state of shock and despair.
